agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with intentionally vague language ('probably', 'competitive but also profitable', 'don't go crazy'), indicating early-stage ideation without firm specifications.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity, used as the primary input signal for price adjustment" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied: the standard delivery price before any dynamic adjustment is applied (not explicitly stated but logically required)" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to a delivery price in response to demand signals, which can be positive (surge) or negative (discount)" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjusted in real time based on current demand levels",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core product behavior — a business policy about how pricing works. The definition of 'real time' and 'demand' are policy decisions that a VP could tune (e.g., change refresh interval, change demand formula).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand level, the system produces an updated price within the defined real-time window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand is high, prices must increase relative to the base price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the fundamental pricing direction policy. A business leader could change the magnitude, curve shape, or thresholds, but the directional relationship is a core product commitment.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand above the high-demand threshold, the calculated price is strictly greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand is low, prices must decrease relative to the base price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Inverse of BR-2. This is a competitive positioning policy — management could decide to hold prices flat at low demand instead of discounting.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand below the low-demand threshold, the calculated price is strictly less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The system must operate fully automated without manual intervention for individual price decisions",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a product design choice. The business could add manual override capabilities or approval workflows at any time.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are calculated and applied without requiring human approval for each transaction"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from exceeding acceptable bounds (both ceiling and floor)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A price without bounds can result in zero or negative prices (destroying revenue) or extreme prices (destroying trust and potentially violating consumer protection laws). The EXISTENCE of guardrails is an invariant — the specific values are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "No dynamically calculated price may be emitted by the system that falls outside the configured guardrail bounds"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The final delivery price must always be greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically nonsensical — the business is performing a service that has real cost. This is a data integrity constraint. Even the most aggressive discount cannot result in paying the customer to deliver.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any combination of demand level and adjustment, the final price output is strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing model must balance competitiveness (not pricing out customers) with profitability (covering costs and generating margin)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a system constraint. The balance point is a policy decision that will shift over time based on market conditions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Not directly testable as stated — requires decomposition into specific margin floors and price ceiling rules (see escalation)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Demand is the primary (and currently only stated) input signal for price adjustment",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The CEO only mentions demand, but other signals (weather, time of day, route distance, competitor pricing) are likely future inputs. This is a policy scope decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "The price adjustment function accepts demand as an input and produces a price adjustment as output"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., concert lets out, natural disaster)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails cap the price at the configured ceiling; the system should not produce a price above the maximum regardless of demand magnitude" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero (no active delivery requests)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails enforce a price floor; the system must not price below the minimum even with zero demand" },
    { "scenario": "Rapid demand oscillation (demand flips between high and low within seconds)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined by spec — risk of price flickering that confuses customers. Likely needs a dampening/smoothing mechanism or minimum price hold duration" },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price, submits order, but demand changed before confirmation", "expected_behavior": "Undefined by spec — critical race condition. Must decide: honor quoted price or apply current price? This has legal and trust implications." },
    { "scenario": "Demand is high in one geographic zone but low in an adjacent zone", "expected_behavior": "Undefined by spec — is demand measured globally, per city, per zone? Geographic granularity is unspecified." },
    { "scenario": "System outage or demand signal unavailable", "expected_behavior": "Undefined by spec — must define fallback pricing behavior. Likely should revert to base price rather than last dynamic price or zero." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail values are misconfigured (e.g., floor > ceiling)", "expected_behavior": "System must reject invalid guardrail configurations and refuse to operate with inconsistent bounds" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-30% during peak demand but risks customer churn if perceived as gouging. Discounting at low demand may increase volume but compress margins. Without clear margin floors, the system could price below cost.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand sensing infrastructure, pricing engine with low latency, and monitoring/alerting for guardrail breaches. Adds operational complexity versus static pricing. Automation reduces manual pricing labor but increases system reliability requirements.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price-sensitive customers may game the system by waiting for low-demand windows. Customers who see price changes between browsing and checkout will have a negative experience.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Price gouging during emergencies may violate consumer protection regulations in some jurisdictions. Fully automated pricing without human oversight creates reputational risk. Vague guardrail definition ('don't go crazy') is insufficient for a production system. Race conditions between price quote and order confirmation create financial exposure.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Variable pricing affects purchasing decisions, perceived fairness, and trust. Must be communicated transparently." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "High-demand pricing may affect their earnings if commission structures are tied to delivery price. Spec does not address this." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Needs visibility into pricing decisions for forecasting, reporting, and margin analysis. Audit trail is implied but not specified." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and fair pricing requirements — especially during emergencies or disasters." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability. Needs tooling to explain why a price was what it was at a given moment." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Pricing signals may shift demand patterns, affecting route optimization and capacity planning." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "The spec is an informal CEO statement from an all-hands meeting, not a product requirements document. Critical parameters are undefined: guardrail values, demand measurement methodology, geographic granularity, price hold duration, fallback behavior, quote-vs-current-price semantics, and the relationship between competitiveness and profitability. The word 'probably' for guardrails is alarming — this is a safety-critical feature being treated as optional. Multiple ambiguities and missing contexts prevent defining reliable acceptance criteria.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices go up when demand is high (BR-2)",
      "Prices go down when demand is low (BR-3)",
      "System is automated (BR-4)",
      "Final price must be positive (BR-6)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'real time' — seconds? minutes?",
      "How demand is measured and what thresholds define high vs low",
      "Specific guardrail bounds (price ceiling and floor values)",
      "Whether guardrails are even confirmed ('probably')",
      "Geographic granularity of demand measurement",
      "Price quote validity window and race condition handling",
      "Fallback behavior during system failures",
      "How courier/driver compensation is affected",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements per jurisdiction",
      "Whether 'competitive' means matching competitors or just not losing customers"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists that dynamic adjustments are applied to",
      "Demand is measurable in a quantifiable, real-time manner",
      "Guardrails will include both a ceiling and a floor despite the CEO saying 'probably'",
      "The pricing applies to delivery fees specifically, not product prices",
      "There is an existing delivery/courier system this integrates into"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrails are described as 'probably' needed. For a fully automated pricing system, guardrails are a safety-critical feature, not optional. The specific ceiling and floor values are completely undefined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["A) Treat guardrails as mandatory with sensible defaults (e.g., 0.5x to 3x base price)", "B) Ship without guardrails and add later", "C) Block launch until guardrail values are defined by Finance and Legal"],
      "recommendation": "Option C. Guardrails must be defined before any implementation begins. Recommend a joint session with Finance (margin floors) and Legal (price gouging ceilings) to set concrete values."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Competitive but also profitable' is a tension without resolution. No margin floor is defined. No competitor benchmarking strategy is defined. The discount floor during low demand could easily price below cost.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance/Revenue Team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["A) Define a hard margin floor (e.g., price must always exceed cost + minimum margin)", "B) Let the algorithm optimize freely within guardrails", "C) Define separate guardrails for surge pricing and discount pricing"],
      "recommendation": "Option A. A hard margin floor is a non-negotiable business invariant. The system must never price a delivery below its cost. Finance must provide the cost model."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of how demand is measured, at what geographic granularity, or what constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand thresholds. These are foundational inputs to the entire system.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations/Dispatch", "Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["A) Define demand as order volume per zone per time window relative to courier availability", "B) Define demand as order volume relative to historical averages", "C) Use a composite signal combining multiple factors"],
      "recommendation": "Need Product and Operations input. Demand definition must account for both request volume AND available supply (courier capacity). A demand-to-supply ratio is more meaningful than absolute volume."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Race condition between price quote and order confirmation is unaddressed. If a customer sees $5 and clicks confirm but demand spiked to $12, which price applies? This has legal and UX implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["A) Honor the quoted price for a defined validity window (e.g., 5 minutes)", "B) Always charge current price at confirmation time with clear disclosure", "C) Show real-time price and require re-confirmation if price changes"],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Industry standard (Uber, Lyft) is to honor the quoted price for a validity window. This protects customer trust and reduces support volume."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback behavior defined for when the pricing system is unavailable or demand signals are missing. A fully automated system with no fallback will either block orders or price at $0.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations/Dispatch", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["A) Fall back to base price when dynamic pricing is unavailable", "B) Fall back to last known dynamic price", "C) Block order placement until pricing is restored"],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Falling back to base price is safe, predictable, and preserves revenue. Option C would cause order loss. Option B risks stale prices from a different demand context."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of audit trail, price explanation, or transparency requirements. Customer support, finance, and regulators may all need to understand why a specific price was charged at a specific moment.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customer Support", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["A) Log every pricing decision with inputs (demand level, timestamp, zone) and output (final price, adjustments applied)", "B) Log only final prices", "C) No logging requirement"],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Full decision logging is essential for dispute resolution, regulatory compliance, and system debugging. This should be a hard requirement."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
